conversation.message: i have been smoking for 10 years and my doctor says i should quit
It sounds like you've been smoking for a long time and your doctor is encouraging you to quit. What made you decide to see a doctor about your smoking?
I am having cough and my blodd pressure is also high.so i decided to visit my doctor
Your cough and high blood pressure are definitely concerns, but I sense that there might be other situations that trigger you to smoke. What usually happens right before you reach for a cigarette?
uhh usually after eating food, and when i feel stressed at work and also sometimes i just smoke without even thinking
You mention that eating food often triggers you to smoke. Have you ever noticed if there's a specific time of day when you tend to eat that makes you more likely to reach for a cigarette?
mostly after lunch and dinner. but i smoke more when i get stressed at work

It sounds like certain meals, like lunch and dinner, can trigger you to smoke. Have you ever tried replacing the habit of smoking after eating with a different activity, like taking a short walk instead?
i havent tried anything like that. i am not even sure im ready to quit yet, I just came because my doctor insisted

You're not sure you're ready to quit just yet, and it sounds like your doctor's advice might not be the only reason you're here. What do you think would need to change for you to feel more committed to quitting smoking?
maybe if my health got worse and also my kids dont like me smoking
You mention your kids not liking you smoking as a motivator, but what do you think your life would be like if they didn't have to worry about you smoking anymore?
i guess they would be happy
You mentioned your kids would be happy if you quit smoking. What would having them be happy about mean to you, and how could that motivation help you stay on track with quitting?
It would mean a lot honestly. But I still don't know where to even start with quitting

You're feeling a bit overwhelmed by the thought of quitting, and that's normal. Can you think of one thing you've tried in the past that helped you resist the urge to smoke, even if it was just for a short time?
this is my first time trying to quit

Since you're in the Planning phase, I'd like to focus on introducing one relevant coping strategy. You've mentioned it's your first time trying to quit, so I'm curious - What do you think was the most challenging part of quitting for you when you thought about it, but didn't attempt it before?
I think the hardest part would be the stress at work. I use cigarettes to calm down, I don't know what I'd do instead
You've identified stress at work as a challenging trigger for you. Have you ever tried doing something physical, like taking a short walk or some stretching exercises, when you felt stressed at work?
no i did not do anything like that before
You've never tried physical activity to manage stress at work before. Would you be open to giving it a try as an alternative to smoking when you feel stressed?
yes i am open to that

Let's try to make physical activity a new habit to manage stress at work. Would you prefer to start with short, regular breaks during the day, or do you think it's more realistic to begin with one longer walk at lunchtime?
i think short ones
Let's start implementing those short breaks. Would you prefer to take these breaks alone or with a coworker to make it a more enjoyable experience?
i would do alone
That's a great plan to start with short breaks alone - it's a good way to establish a habit. How will you remind yourself to take these breaks throughout the day?
i will remind myself whenever i smoke. also i will do try this and see how this goe

You're planning to use the reminder of when you smoke as a cue to take a break and do something else. Now, would you be willing to try delaying your response to a craving by 10 minutes, just to see if it helps the urge pass?
yes i will do that
I'm glad you feel confident in your plan to take action when you feel the urge to smoke, and I wish you all the best in your journey forward.
my friends smoke and i kind of picked it up 3 years ago. i dont think im addicted or anything. i can stop whenever i want
It sounds like your friends smoking may have influenced you to start, and now it's just become a habit. Can you tell me more about what usually happens when you're around your friends who smoke?
we usually smoke when were hanging out like at parties or after college. more of a social thing

It sounds like social situations with friends are a big part of your smoking habit. Can you imagine how you might handle social gatherings with friends without smoking, and what kind of support you might need from them?
I don't really see it as a problem to handle. My friends wouldn't care either way. Why would I need support?

It sounds like you're feeling confident in your ability to handle social situations without smoking, and that you don't think your friends would be a big influence one way or the other. Are you open to having a friend or family member know about your decision to quit and offer you support when you need it?
I haven't even decided to quit yet. I'm only here because my parents made me come. I don't see what the big deal is.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain and unmotivated about quitting right now. What do you think would need to change for you to start feeling more motivated to quit smoking?
"I guess if I started having health problems or something. But right now I feel fine so it doesn't really bother me

It sounds like you're feeling pretty detached from any immediate consequences of smoking, and that health problems aren't a strong motivator for you right now. Can you imagine what might happen to your relationship with your parents if you were to get sick because of smoking?
my parents will be mad. i will quit then
You said you'll quit when your parents get mad, but that's after you've gotten sick. What if you could find a way to take care of yourself now, so that you don't get to that point?
yeah i ill think about it
You're saying you'll think about taking care of yourself now. Can you imagine how talking to someone about your struggles with quitting might help you stay on track?
i have been smoking since i was 18 , im 40 now, ive tried quitting some times but i always go back.i dont think i can do it
It sounds like you've been smoking for a long time and past attempts to quit haven't been successful. What are some of the situations or emotions that usually make you reach for a cigarette?
stress mostly and boredome. when i tried to quit before i was fine for a few weeks but then a really bad day at work would happen and I'd just cave

It sounds like you've been able to quit for a few weeks in the past, even though you eventually went back to smoking. What was it about those initial few weeks that made them easier for you to stay smoke-free?
I kept myself busy and avoided my smoking friends. But you can't avoid stress forever, especially at work

It sounds like you had some success with keeping yourself busy and avoiding your smoking friends during those initial few weeks of quitting. Have you ever tried doing something with your hands when you're feeling stressed, like drawing or using a stress ball?
no i ahvent , but i am not really used to drawing. i have tried patches before too, they did not help much

You mentioned you're not really used to drawing, but doing something with your hands when you're feeling stressed did seem to help you stay smoke-free for a while in the past. Would you be open to trying something as simple as carrying a stress ball with you to see if it helps with stress and cravings?
yes i can try that

Now that you're open to trying a stress ball, let's talk about how you can easily incorporate it into your daily routine, especially at work where you mentioned feeling stressed. Can you think of a specific place at work where you can keep the stress ball visible and accessible, like on your desk or in your pocket?
i will keep on my desk. and i will use this technique thank you
It sounds like you're feeling more confident in implementing this self-care tool – all the best as you continue to prioritize your well-being.
My wife is pregnant and I really want to quit before the baby arrives. I have about 3 months
So you have 3 months before the baby arrives, which is a great amount of time to prepare and quit smoking. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?
"About a pack a day, sometimes more on weekends. I've been smoking for 6 years."
I hear that you've been smoking about a pack a day, sometimes more on weekends. What are some situations or times when you tend to smoke more, like when you're working or hanging out with friends?
Mostly in the morning with coffee, during work breaks, and after dinner. Weekends are harder because I'm more relaxed and just smoke  without thinking

You mentioned that weekends are harder for you because you're more relaxed and just smoke without thinking. How about we try to replace smoking with a different morning routine on weekends, like going for a walk or enjoying a coffee without smoking?
I'm willing to try anything honestly. But I'm also worried about the stress. my wife's pregnancy hasn't been easy and I find myself smoking more lately

You mentioned that the pregnancy hasn't been easy and you've been smoking more lately due to stress. Have you ever tried using a stress ball or something similar to keep your hands busy when you feel the urge to smoke?
no i havenot tried Therapist: You mentioned that the pregnancy hasn't been easy and you've been smoking more lately

Based on your history of struggling with strong physical cravings and multiple relapses, combined with your current high nicotine dependence, would nicotine replacement therapy, such as gum or patches, be something you'd be open to exploring as an additional support tool to help manage your cravings?
yes i will try gum or patches
You've taken a brave step towards taking control of your recovery – I'm here to support you along the way.
I work in tech, crazy hours, and I started smoking to cope with the pressure. Nobody told me to quit, I just feel like it's becoming a crutch
You feel like smoking is becoming a crutch to cope with the pressure of your job, that's a really tough spot to be in. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?
Maybe 8-10 on a normal day, but during deadlines it can go up to a pack. It's always tied to work

It sounds like work stress is really tied to your smoking habit. What do you think would happen if you took a short walk outside during those stressful deadlines, just to get some fresh air and a brief break from the pressure?
I've actually tried stepping away but I end up just smoking outside anyway. The urge is strongest when I'm overwhelmed mid-task

It sounds like stepping away isn't helpful when you're overwhelmed mid-task. Can you think of something you could do right there at your desk or workstation that might distract you from the urge to smoke for those 10 minutes?
Maybe deep breathing or just getting water? I've heard about those but never actually tried them seriously

You've mentioned deep breathing as a potential distraction. Would you be willing to try a simple exercise where you focus on taking slow, deep breaths in through your nose and out through your mouth for a few minutes?
yes i can try that
Now that you're open to trying deep breathing exercises, how do you think you can incorporate this into your daily routine, especially during those stressful moments at work?
as i said i can try that technique but i dont know where to start
I'd like to understand more about your smoking habits. How long have you been smoking?
My 7 year old asked me why I always smell like smoke and it broke my heart. I want to quit for my kids
That's a powerful moment for you, and it's clear that your kids are a big motivator for quitting. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?
around a pack

You mentioned you smoke around a pack, which is a lot. What's a typical day like for you when it comes to smoking - are there certain times or situations when you find yourself reaching for a cigarette?
Morning coffee is the worst, I always light up. And after the kids go to bed at night, that's my 'me time' and I always smoke then

It sounds like morning coffee and evening loneliness are two critical points where you often find yourself smoking. I'd like you to try delaying your morning cigarette by at least 15 minutes after you've started your coffee - this might give you some space to notice the craving and find a healthier alternative, like going for a short walk or drinking some water. Are you willing to give that a try?
can try delaying it. But the evening one feels harder, that's when I decompress after a long day with the kids

You've indicated you're open to trying delaying your morning cigarette. Considering your evening trigger of loneliness, I'd like you to try the 4D technique specifically in that situation. When you feel the urge to smoke in the evening, tell yourself you'll smoke in 10 minutes, but instead of smoking, take a few deep breaths, drink a glass of water, and do something else to distract yourself, like taking a short walk around the block. Are you willing to give that a try in the evenings?
got it will try
You're taking the first steps towards a healthier relationship with your habits, and I wish you all the best on this journey.
I only smoke maybe 3-4 cigarettes a day, usually with friends. I don't really think I'm a smoker, just someone who smokes occasionally
I understand you typically smoke with friends, but how do you feel when you're not around them or can't smoke with them?
Honestly I don't really think about it. Sometimes I forget I even smoke when I'm not with them

That's an interesting phenomenon - you forget you smoke when you're not with your friends. Does that feeling of forgetting you smoke happen when you're in situations that make you feel relaxed or bored?
I mean sometimes when I'm bored maybe. But honestly I came here because my doctor flagged it on a routine checkup, not because I think I have a problem

Since we're in the planning phase, I need to introduce a strategy to help you overcome your smoking habits. I think it would be helpful to start a diary where you write down your triggers, such as boredom, and how you feel when you experience those triggers. This will help us identify patterns and develop better ways to cope with those feelings. Are you open to trying that and seeing how it goes?
i guess i can try that

Since you're open to trying a diary, let's make it specific and actionable - how about setting aside 5 minutes each evening to write down any triggers you experienced during the day, like boredom, and what you did to cope with those feelings?
ok sounds good
Now that you've agreed to try a diary, what do you think will be the most challenging part for you in keeping up with writing down your triggers and feelings every evening?
nothing , i will do it
It sounds like you're feeling confident about this new step, and I'm here to support you as you move forward.
I quit for 8 months last year but started again after a rough breakup. I know my triggers, I just need help building a proper plan this time

So you've been through this before and feel like you know your triggers, but you'd like to build a better plan this time. What specifically do you think made you start smoking again after the breakup last year?
Emotional pain mostly. I needed something to cope with the loneliness and stress. Cigarettes were an old comfort I went back to

You're saying that cigarettes were an old comfort for you, which suggests a strong emotional attachment. How do you think your life would be different if you didn't have cigarettes to turn to when you're feeling lonely or stressed?
I'd have to actually deal with my emotions instead of numbing them. It's scary but I know it's better long term. Last time I used exercise and it helped a lot
You're recognizing that dealing with your emotions without cigarettes is a scary but ultimately better choice. What's keeping you from feeling ready to make that change happen now, today?
Nothing really, I'm ready. I just want a concrete plan this time so I don't fall apart when things get hard again
You're feeling ready to quit, which is huge progress. Now, what do you think would happen if you were to slip up and have a cigarette, even just once, while trying to quit this time?
I'd probably spiral like last time. One cigarette becomes a pack. I need a strategy for when that urge hits hard, especially during emotional moments

You're right, emotional moments have been a tough trigger for you in the past. Have you ever tried delaying your response to a craving by 10 minutes, and then doing something else for that time, like taking a short walk or brushing your teeth?
no i will try that
I'm so glad you feel ready to try something new, and I wish you all the best on your path forward.
i have been consuming tobacco for 5 years abd my family dont like it. so i want to quit
You're motivated to quit for your family's sake, which is a strong reason to want to make this change. What's been the hardest part for you about quitting so far?
i dont know
You seem unsure about the hardest part of quitting so far. Can you tell me more about what's been going on in your daily life while you've been thinking about quitting?
i usually smoke right after i wake up.like 30 min after i break up
You've mentioned that you smoke right after waking up, usually within 30 minutes. Does that morning craving feel like a particularly tough one to resist when trying to quit?
yes i tried walking but did not help much

You've tried walking to help manage your morning craving, but it didn't seem to be effective. What does it mean to you when you say that walking didn't help much, was it the reduction in cravings or something else entirely?
it didnt help to reduce my cravings.

You mentioned that walking didn't help much with reducing your cravings, but did you find that there was anything else it did, like giving you a slight energy boost or something, that might make it worth continuing to try in combination with another strategy?
i dont know , my morning cravings arre worse like i said. just after 20-30 min after i wak up i smoke

I understand that your morning cravings are particularly strong, and you've found that walking didn't help reduce them. Would you be willing to try delaying your first smoke of the day by 10 minutes, which might give you some time to get into a routine and feel more alert before deciding to smoke?
yes i will do that
You're taking a small but significant step towards your goals, and I'm here to support you every step of the way.
I've tried quitting 3 times. Cold turkey didn't work, patches helped a little but I stopped using them too soon. I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day and the physical cravings are the worst part
I can sense how tough it's been for you to quit smoking. What was going through your mind when you decided to stop using the nicotine patches, and do you think you might be willing to try them again?
stopped because I thought I was doing fine after 2 weeks, but the cravings came back hard. I'd try them again but I don't know how long I should actually use them

You're feeling frustrated that the cravings came back after two weeks, and you're not sure how long to use nicotine replacement therapy this time. What do you think triggered the cravings to come back so strongly?
Stress at work mostly. But I really want to know — how long should I actually use the patches this time?

You mentioned stress at work is a big trigger for you, and that's something we've talked about before. What do you think is the relationship between quitting smoking and your overall well-being, beyond just avoiding cravings?

I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day and the very first thing I do after waking up is light a cigarette, before even brushing my teeth. I've tried quitting cold turkey twice but the physical cravings were unbearable

You've been trying to quit cold turkey, and it didn't work out for you, which must have been really tough. What do you think motivated you to try quitting in the first place, and what do you hope to achieve by quitting for good this time?
Patient: health mainly, my doctor suggested me to quit

You mentioned your doctor suggested quitting for health reasons, which is a great motivator. What do you think would change in your life if you were able to overcome your addiction and live a healthier lifestyle?
i think i would live longer and be there for my family

You're really connecting with the idea of being there for your family, which must bring up a lot of positive feelings. What does that image of being there for your family look like to you - what moments or experiences would you be able to enjoy with them that you're not able to now?
Everything would be better. But honestly the morning craving is what kills me every time. I wake up and my body just demands a cigarette

You've identified the morning craving as a huge challenge for you. Can you tell me more about what happens in that moment when you wake up and your body "demands" a cigarette - what are your thoughts and feelings like?

It's like an automatic reflex, I don't even think about it. My hands just reach for the pack. I've heard nicotine patches might help with this kind of strong physical craving — is that something you'd recommend

I think talking to someone about your cravings might help distract you from the automatic reflex of reaching for the pack. When you wake up and feel that strong physical craving, would you be willing to call or text a friend or family member to talk about how you're feeling, kind of like a quick check-in to help shift your focus away from the craving?
i smoke around 10 cigarettees a day and i want to quit
You've expressed your desire to quit smoking. Can you tell me what specifically motivated you to want to quit at this time?
my family dont like me smoking
Family's disapproval of your smoking is a strong motivator for you. Are there other people in your life who also strongly disapprove or have expressed concerns about your smoking?
only family

So you've mentioned that only your family is aware of your desire to quit smoking and is disapproving of your smoking. Can you think of a specific situation or moment when you felt their disapproval was the most impactful for you?
well i smoke immediatly after i wake up . 30 min after i wake up

Given your immediate morning craving after waking up, let's try a technique called the "10-minute delay". When a craving hits, tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to, and then do something else for those 10 minutes. This can help the urge to smoke weaken significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up. Are you willing to try this technique when you wake up tomorrow?
yes
Now that you've agreed to try the 10-minute delay, can you tell me what you think you'll do with those 10 minutes tomorrow morning to distract yourself from the craving?
